title: How CBT sessions work in practice
citation: Clark, D.M. (1989). Anxiety states: Panic and generalized anxiety. In K. Hawton, P.M. Salkovskis, J. Kirk, & D.M. Clark (Eds.), Cognitive behaviour therapy for psychiatric problems: A practical guide. Oxford University Press.
content:
Walking into a first CBT session can feel uncertain, especially if you have never had therapy before. Understanding what to expect can ease some of that uncertainty. CBT sessions are structured by design, which many people find reassuring rather than rigid.

Most sessions begin with a brief check-in and then an agreed agenda. The therapist will ask what you want to focus on today, and together you will set one or two specific goals for the hour. This collaborative agenda-setting ensures the session stays useful and relevant to your current experience rather than wandering without direction.

A significant part of CBT involves working with thought records. These are simple tools, often completed as written exercises, in which you capture a situation that triggered distress, identify the thoughts that went through your mind, note how those thoughts made you feel, and then examine the evidence for and against the thought. Over time, this process helps you spot habitual patterns of thinking and consider more balanced alternatives.

Behavioural experiments are another key technique. These involve testing out a belief in real life rather than just discussing it. For example, if you believe that speaking up in a meeting will lead to humiliation, a behavioural experiment might involve doing exactly that and observing what actually happens. The evidence gathered from lived experience tends to be far more persuasive than reassurance alone.

At the end of each session, the therapist will typically summarise what was covered and agree on between-session tasks. These tasks, sometimes called homework, are not meant to be burdensome. They are practical extensions of what was explored in the session, designed to give you the repetition and real-world practice that drives lasting change.

CBT is collaborative and requires active participation, but it is also bounded and time-limited. Most people complete a course within a matter of weeks or months, leaving with tools they can continue to use independently long after therapy has ended.
